coredata.dc:title: Multiplex recognition and logic devices for molecular robot prototype based on an europium(iii)–cyclen system
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hao
Cao, Jing
Zhou, Panpan
Li, Xiang
Xie, Yujie
Liu, Weisheng
Tang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Molecular robot provides a promising way to receive response signals and make decisions with intelligent information processing at the molecule level. In this work, a molecular robot prototype based on a europium(III)-cyclen complex (EuL) was designed and assembled, consisting of multiplex sensing and information processing in a single system. By utilizing the Eu3+ ion as a central processing unit (CPU) and terpyridine (Terpy) group as an arm of the molecular robot, multiplex detection function of this molecular robot can be accomplished in three test channels: the detection of Fe2+ with UV–vis, Zn2+ using photoluminescence, and Mn2+ using magnetic resonance imaging. The optimized structures revealed that the transition metal ions bound by the arms of two EuL molecules formed a hexacoordination octahedral rigidity structure in the adducts. For the structure of L and EuL, three pyridine rings of Terpy unit were not in a plane, and they were fixed to form a plane when captured the transition metal ions during the formation of the adducts. In addition, three different modes of molecular logic devices (OR, INHIBIT, and YES) were established based on relevant signals in the multiplex detection channels. The sensing of metal ions in human urine using EuL was also carried out, and the result demonstrated the presence of good recoveries and high analytical precision. Such a molecular robot prototype is expected to be used in intelligent medical diagnostics and, in particular, information processing at molecular level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiplex recognition
Logic devices
Molecular robot prototype
Medical diagnostics
Information processing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis absorption spectra of EuL (20 µM) in the presence of various metal ions (1 equiv. K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+) in an HNO3-Tris (50 mM, pH 7.0) solution. Inset: the color images of EuL (left) and the [Fe( EuL )2](NO3)8 complex (right) solutions (20 µM). b) UV–vis titration of EuL (20 µM) with Fe2+ at room temperature. The arrows indicate the direction of absorbance with increasing concentration of Fe2+ (From 0.5–10 µM, interval: 0.5 µM). Inset: The absorbance at 569 nm varied as a function of [Fe2+]/[ EuL ]. c) The normalized absorbance intensity at 569 nm of EuL (20 µM) in the presence of various metal ions (0.5 equiv.). Inset: The mechanism of UV–vis detection of Fe2+ at 569 nm. d) The Job plot showing 2:1 binding of EuL to Fe2+. The total [ EuL ] + [Fe2+] = 10 µM.

a) Schematic energy level diagram and the energy transfer process of EuL ; b) PL spectra of EuL (20 µM) upon addition of metal ions at room temperature. Inset: fluorescence change image under UV lamp (365 nm) before and after the addition of Zn2+; c) Luminescence emission intensity at 617 nm of EuL (20 µM) in the presence of various metal ions (0.5 equiv.); d) Luminescence lifetime decay profiles of metal ions coordination with EuL in the solution.

a) Relaxivity responses of EuL (0.2 mM) to various metal ions and T1-weighted phantom MRI images. Inset: from left to right and up to down: K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+; b) Relaxivity titration of EuL with Mn2+ in HNO3-Tris (50 mM, pH 7.0) solution and corresponding T1-weighted phantom MRI images.
The optimized geometries of L (a), EuL (b), [Fe( EuL )2](NO3)8 (c), [Zn( EuL )2](NO3)8 (d), [Mn( EuL )2](NO3)8 (e).
The three molecular logic gates (OR, INH, YES) constructed by EuL , including symbolisation, combinatorial logic scheme, and truth table.
a) The synthesis route of EuL (the N in Terpy is specified by the digital number (1, 2, 3) in the right of EuL molecule); b) the three detection channel of EuL .

head.ce:title: Multiplex recognition and logic devices for molecular robot prototype based on an europium(iii)–cyclen system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecular robot provides a promising way to receive response signals and make decisions with intelligent information processing at the molecule level. In this work, a molecular robot prototype based on a europium(III)-cyclen complex (EuL) was designed and assembled, consisting of multiplex sensing and information processing in a single system. By utilizing the Eu3+ ion as a central processing unit (CPU) and terpyridine (Terpy) group as an arm of the molecular robot, multiplex detection function of this molecular robot can be accomplished in three test channels: the detection of Fe2+ with UV–vis, Zn2+ using photoluminescence, and Mn2+ using magnetic resonance imaging. The optimized structures revealed that the transition metal ions bound by the arms of two EuL molecules formed a hexacoordination octahedral rigidity structure in the adducts. For the structure of L and EuL, three pyridine rings of Terpy unit were not in a plane, and they were fixed to form a plane when captured the transition metal ions during the formation of the adducts. In addition, three different modes of molecular logic devices (OR, INHIBIT, and YES) were established based on relevant signals in the multiplex detection channels. The sensing of metal ions in human urine using EuL was also carried out, and the result demonstrated the presence of good recoveries and high analytical precision. Such a molecular robot prototype is expected to be used in intelligent medical diagnostics and, in particular, information processing at molecular level.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular robots (Hagiya et al., 2014; Erbas-Cakmak et al., 2015; Dey and Sen, 2017) have drawn dramatically increased attentions since it provides a promising way to receive response signals (Douglas et al., 2012; Abendroth et al., 2015; Kassem et al., 2016) and make decisions (Reif, 2011) with intelligent information processing at the molecule level. In particular, simulation of molecular robots to build an autonomous molecular device with multi-functional biological recognition and intelligent analysis has become a hot and challenging research in chemistry and biology fields (Li et al., 2017a, 2017b, 2017c). Tremendous efforts from the molecular robots researchers have greatly improved the power, functionality versatility, and capabilities of the various molecular robots prototype (Lund et al., 2010). The growing complexity of these molecular robots offers great potential for various biomedical applications.

During the exploring of the molecular robots possessing smart multi-functional sensing, the construction of perfect multiplex recognition architecture which can implement detection and quantification of multi-analytes in competitive assays is a focus filed. It also offered a new paradigm for future computing technology in light of the need for miniaturization and the increase of function density. The research on multi-functional molecular probes has been seeking to overcome the boundary of single analyte detection and to initiate highly selective orthogonal signaling protocols (logic gate) for multianalyte mixtures and to exploit their application as computation and analytical devices (Schmittel and Lin, 2007). Thus, the design and development of new molecular unit capable of achieving multiplex recognition is very crucial to assemble the autonomous molecular system. Up to now, a series of multiplex recognition systems have been successfully designed and reported (Chen and Schmittel, 2014; Hettie et al., 2014; Reddy et al., 2017; Kovacs et al., 2017). However, as for the more complicated multi-component biological environment, it still remains a huge challenge how to accomplish intelligent information processing in addition to the high sensitive and selective recognition function, and greater stability of organic or metal-organic compounds in aqueous solution. Hence, designing a molecular robot prototype to resolve above mentioned issues at the molecule level is highly expected.

Lanthanide complexes show their distinct advantages in assembly of molecular sensors with abundant ligand design (Binnemans, 2009; Heffern et al., 2014; G. Bünzli, 2010; Shao et al., 2008; Li et al., 2017a, 2017b, 2017c; Liu et al., 2013a, 2013b), flexible coordination modes, high 4 f single-electron for magnetic resonance imaging (Wu et al., 2010; Tu et al., 2009), and excellent luminescent properties such as line-like emissions, large Stokes shift, and long-live lifetimes (Li et al., 2017a, 2017b, 2017c; Chen et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2017). These special properties could be well matched with the requirement of multiplex recognition and intelligent computers of molecular robots by the molecular design. Up to now, a series of lanthanide-based molecular sensors have been successfully designed by Gunnlaugsson (Surender et al., 2017; Gunnlaugsson et al., 2001; Bradberry et al., 2015) and other groups (Bui et al., 2017; Feng et al., 2016; Louie, 2010; Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b). However, it is still difficult to construct lanthanide-based molecular robots with multiplex sensing and logic devices couple with information processing in a molecular system.

Bearing these points in mind, the principal objective of the present work is in designing a molecular robot prototype based on a europium(III)-cyclen complex ( EuL ). As shown in Scheme 1, the designed EuL is applicable for efficient detection of three main transition metal ions in aqueous solution, with involvement of molecular logic devices (OR, INHIBIT, and YES logic gates). In principle, a molecular robot should contain a single type of receptor unit for multi-ions analyses depending on array of detection methods, as well as output signals responding in the sensing channels. Based on this guideline, the molecular robot prototype of the europium(III)-cyclen complex was designed and synthesized to satisfy the requirements: 1) the terpyridine (Terpy) unit similar to an arm of a robot, has been developed as an excellent receptor for transition metal ions, inducing the steric perturbations and conformational constraints, also the unit is an excellent chromophore and a good antenna for europium(III); 2) the cyclen unit increases the water-solubility of organic ligand and affords a desired coordination environment for europium(III) with high coordination number; 3) europium(III), as the central processing unit (CPU) of the molecular robot, has potential signal output in photoluminescence (PL) imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Therefore, the signal of the designed molecular robot could be tuned by the coordination function of the mechanical arm grasping with the transition metal ions. To prove the validity of this concept, the sensing properties of this rudiment of molecular robot were tested in three channels (UV–vis, PL, and MRI) using the following array of bio-cations: K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, which played important roles in life system. Moreover, three modes of logic devices (OR, INHIBIT, and YES) were constructed using the selected test ions as input signal of logic operations, whereas the detection channels could be regarded as output signals. By reasonable molecular design including chip and arm, a new molecular robot prototype based on the europium(III)-cyclen complex was successfully constructed to achieve multi-analyte intelligent detection in aqueous solution and information processing at the molecular level. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of molecular robot prototype based on an ingenious lanthanide complex. Such a molecular robot prototype could inspire new designs with intelligent multiple sensing and information processing functions for medical diagnostics or therapeutics.

2-Chloroacetyl chloride, 2-acetylpyridine, 4-methylbenzaldehyde, and cyclen (1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) was obtained by dissolving Eu2O3 (99.99%, Shanghai Yuelong) in nitric acid followed by successive fuming to remove excess acid. The other chemicals were all commercially available. All of the chemicals were used as received.

The longitudinal relaxation times (T1) of aqueous buffer solutions of EuL were measured by a standard inversion recovery pulse sequence on a PQ-001 NMR Analyzing & Imaging system (Shanghai Niumag Corporation) at 0.5 T. The relaxivity r1 is commonly used to express the ability of proton relaxation enhancement of a paramagnetic compound.

The UV–vis detection technology is widely used due to its simplicity and convenience, especially for the naked eye observation. Terpy unit is an excellent chromophore in UV–vis range. As shown in Fig. 1a, the absorption spectrum of EuL presented two band centred at 276 and 323 nm, corresponding to the π-π * and n-π*transition of the Terpy moiety, respectively (Senechal-David et al., 2006; Schott et al., 2014; Schütte et al., 1998). On addition of Fe2+, a strong absorption band in the visible region was found centred at 569 nm (Fig. 1b), which was characteristic of the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) transition of the iron(II)-Terpy complex. In the UV region, the peak at 276 nm decreased gradually, and the peak at 323 nm increased with the addition of Fe2+, mainly due to the change of electronic structure and enhancement of the rigidity of the Terpy unit by capturing the Fe2+ ion. The absorbance of EuL (20 µM) exhibited good selectivity toward Fe2+ over other metal ions (Fig. 1c and Fig. S1). The Job plot (Fig. 1d) unveiled a 1:2 stoichiometry for the formation of adduct [Fe( EuL )2](NO3)8 with a stability constant K B = 4.2 × 1010 M-2 (the inset of Fig. 1b) (Que et al., 2015; Benesi and Hildebrand, 1949). Therefore, as indicated in Fig. 1c, we speculated that two Terpy units coordinated with Fe2+ ion were vertical cross to each other. A relationship (linear: 0–8.5 µM) allowed quantification of Fe2+ at a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.36 µM (Fig. S2). Moreover, the color change that was identified by absorption spectra can be well recognized by the naked eyes, even at a Fe2+ concentration as low as 20 µM (the inset of Fig. 1a).

Fluorescence detection technology has attracted much attention in various fields owing to its unique properties, such as high sensitivity, quick response, and low LOD. Except an excellent receptor for transition metal ions, the Terpy unit is also a good antenna for Eu3+. In order to study the luminescence property of EuL , we firstly investigated the energy-level of the ligand L in the GdL complex.41 To elucidate the energy transfer process in EuL , the singlet energy level of the ligand L was determined by the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the complex GdL in HNO3-Tris buffered aqueous solution (pH = 7.0) at room temperature (Fig. S3). The wavelength of the absorbance edge of complex is 345 nm, indicating that the singlet excited energy level of the ligand is 3.59 eV. Furthermore, in term of the low-temperature (77 K) phosphorescence spectrum of GdL , we calculated the triplet excited energy level (2.77 eV) of L (Fig. S4). According to Latva's empirical rule that an optimal ligand-to-metal energy transfer process for Ln3+ needs 0.31–0.50 eV for Eu3+, the triplet excited energy level (3ππ*) is suitable for the energy transfer to the 5D0 level of Eu3+ ions (2.15 eV). Therefore, the cyclen-Terpy ligand is a suitable antenna for Eu3+ ions ( Fig. 2a).

To study the selectivity toward above-mentioned bio-cations in luminescence channel, the PL spectra of EuL were measured immediately for the addition of 0.5 equiv. metal ion. Among various bio-cations, only Zn2+ induced a prominent luminescence enhancement at the same concentration (Figs. 2b and 2c), whereas other interferences resulted in negligible luminescence changes (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+) or luminescence quenching (Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+). The emission peaks at 580, 594, and 617 nm were attributed to the characteristic transitions of Eu3+ ions, from the 5D0 energy level to 7FJ multiplets (J = 0, 1, and 2), respectively (Fig. 2b). The enhanced absorption of EuL complex was observed at 325 nm (Fig. S5), which was the excited wavelength of EuL , corresponding to the n-π* transition of the Terpy moiety. Addition of a small amount of aqueous Zn2+ solution enhanced the emission intensity of Eu3+ ions (Fig. 2b) based on efficient energy transfer from the ligand to europium ions, which can be ascribed to the enhancement of conjugacy of the ligand after the introduction of Zn2+ ions. Interference experiments (Fig. S6) revealed that distinct PL effects were not observed upon addition of 0.5 equiv. other biological metal ions (rather than Zn2+) to the solution of EuL .

In a titration study (Fig. S7), EuL exhibited a 7.5-fold enhancement of Eu3+ emission (617 nm) with addition of 0.5 equiv. of Zn2+ ions, and the luminescence titration plot showed a 2:1 binding stoichiometry. A linear response was observed over the concentration range of 0–8 µM zinc ion. The PL signal was observed to stabilize immediately (Zhou et al., 2014), which was a highly desirable feature from the viewpoint of developing a rapid assay for zinc ion measurement. The LOD of EuL to Zn2+ was determined to be 16 nM, which was comparable to the previous work (Que et al., 2015). And the stability constant of the adduct of Zn2+ with EuL was calculated to be 5.6 × 1011 M-2 from emission titration data (Benesi and Hildebrand, 1949).

To further understand the selectivity of EuL , the lifetimes of the excited state 5D0 (Eu3+) of the complex were monitored with and without addition of metal ions at the same conditions (Fig. 2d). The lifetime of EuL is 617.0 µs. Five metal ions, including K+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, were selected as examples. The lifetime of [Zn( EuL )2](NO3)8 increased to 816.0 µs upon addition of Zn2+ (Table S1), well correlated with increase of luminescence intensity shown in Figs. 2b and 2c. The addition of three metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+) can decrease the lifetime of EuL to 255.0, 401.4, 404.2 µs, respectively, owing to single electron in 3d orbit. As expected, K+ ion can lead to negligible changes of lifetime for EuL . From above discussion, we speculate that the anti-magnetic Zn2+ ion is able to enhance the luminescence of EuL through improving the rigidity of the antenna, while other paramagnetic transition metal ions, including Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, induced PL quenching of EuL .

Compared with the two modes of above mentioned UV–vis and PL, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has become increasingly popular in experimental molecular imaging and clinical laboratory because it is noninvasive and capable of producing three-dimensional representations of opaque organisms with high spatial and temporal resolution (Luo et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012; Que and Chang, 2010; Amoroso and Pope, 2015; Zhu et al., 2014; Ding et al., 2016). The ability of Mn2+ to modulate the longitudinal relaxivity of EuL was determined using T1 measurements at a proton frequency of 20 MHz (pH 7.0). In the absence of Mn2+, the relaxivity of EuL (0.2 mM) was 1.06 mM-1 s-1, which gradually increased to 5.56 mM-1 s-1 with the addition of 0.5 equiv. Mn2+, whereas other cations led to negligible changes ( Fig. 3). The absorption spectra of EuL complex also showed an enhancement at 325 nm with the addition of Mn2+ (Fig. S8), which was consistent with the phenomenon with Fe2+ and Zn2+, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the Terpy moiety. Adding Mn2+ triggered about 5.24-fold enhancement in the relaxivity, and the enhancement is higher than previously reported Gd3+ complex (Zhang et al., 2012). The augment of relaxivity for EuL inducing by Mn2+ addition are stable over time, indicating that the complex has excellent stability and remains intact in the presence of Mn2+. Also, the acquired T1-weighted images of EuL (20 µM) with a commercial 0.5 T magnet in the absence and presence of Mn2+ ions (10 µM) readily visualized differences in the manganese levels in a biologically relevant micromolar range. It is worth mentioning that manganese is a nutrient essential to the function of several enzymes and physiological processes.

To detect selectivity and tolerance of EuL in response to Mn2+, the relaxivity measurements were carried out in the presence of other biological metal ions. The results indicated that the relaxivity changes of EuL were due to Mn2+ specificity over other physiologically important metal ions (Fig. S9) including Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+ and Ni2+. Even upon the addition of 0.2 mM (10 equiv.) of metal ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, and K+, the relaxivity of EuL changed insignificantly. Interestingly, EuL exhibited high excellent selectivity toward Mn2+ over Cu2+, which was unprecedented compared with previously reported works.43 Thus, EuL exhibited high selectivity in response to Mn2+ with remarkable enhancement of relaxivity, which was almost undisturbed by other biological metal ions. The relaxivity of EuL exhibited a linear response to Mn2+ concentration in the range of 0–10 µM (Fig. 3b), from which the LOD for Mn2+ was experimentally determined to be 0.1 µM (R = 0.9906). By fitting the relaxivity intensity data to the Benesi-Hildebrand expression, the stability constant value of the adduct was determined to be 2.6 × 1010 M-2, which was well correlated with the binding constant (1.4 × 1010 M-2) calculated based on the UV–vis titration (Fig. S8). And an obvious linear variation of MRI responses was observed with a pH range from 6.0 to 9.0 (Fig. S10) (Gunnlaugsson and Leonard, 2005; Wu et al., 2010; Kong et al., 2012).

To establish the mechanism, luminescence decay rates (Horrocks and Sudnick, 1981) of the europium(III) complex in water (H2O) and heavy water (D2O) with and without manganese(II) were measured to calculate the number of inner-sphere water molecules (q; Table S2). The change of q from 0.85 (in the absence of Mn2+) to 1.25 (in the addition of Mn2+) confirmed that Mn2+ binding to Terpy group increased the number of the inner-sphere water molecules unconspicuously. Of interest was the relaxivity increased 5.24-fold. There are two possible reasons to further understand the increase of the relaxivity. Firstly, as explained in literature, a larger rotational tumbling time (tR) induces a higher relaxivity (Que et al., 2010), which is positively related to its molecular mass. The molecular mass changed from ( EuL ) to [Mn( EuL )2] when coordinated with Mn2+. Secondly, it is also an important reason that Mn2+ (3d5) possess more unpaired electrons than other ions. As also proved in previous work (Vaddypally et al., 2013), manga-nese terpy complex often exhibits the octahedron characteristic of a six-coordinated high-spin (S = 5/2) Mn(II). Thus, the factors that Mn2+ contributes to the larger relax-ivity enhancement for EuL than other cations include the more single-electron numbers, the increased molecular weight and molecular rigidity of the adduct.

In addition, we further assessed the structural features of the ligand L , the complex EuL and the adducts M( EuL )2 (M= Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Mn2+) by theoretical calculations. In the optimized structure of the ligand L and the complex EuL , we can clearly identify that three pyridine rings of Terpy group were not in a plane ( Fig. 4), while they were in a plane when transition metal ions were added to form the adducts [M( EuL )2](NO3)8 (Fig. 4 and Fig. S11). As shown in Table S3, the length of N1-M bond is shorter than other N-M bonds. The two Terpy units are vertical cross to each other, which is in good consistent with the previous conjecture based on experiment results indicated in Fig. 1c. Therefore, in these structures, the transition metal ions adopt a distorted octahedral geometry with a meridional arrangement of the two tridentate Terpy units (Pal et al., 2014). That is, only one metal ion occupies the coordination center assembled by two EuL molecules and the hexacoordination octahedral structure is formed by the two Terpy units coordinating with one cation.

On the basis of above experiments, we have proved the feasibility of EuL for multi-ion analysis by using an array of detection methods. Apart from multi-ion analysis, molecular logic gates (Li et al., 2012; de Silva et al., 1993; Margulies et al., 2005; Deng et al., 2016; Tam et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2011) are also an important part of molecular robots. To accomplish the building of molecular robots, we also constructed three modes of molecular logic gates (OR, INH, YES) based on previous multi-ion analysis (UV–vis, PL, MR), respectively. Take the INH molecular logic gates as example, the PL emission intensity at the 617 nm continuously increased with increasing H+ concentration. And the PL emission intensity showed good linearity with pH variation in the range of 6.5–9.0 (Fig. S12). The linear range was wider than previously reported pH probes and met the requirement for in vivo imaging and sensing of pH.

In addition, the change of PL intensity based on the pH variation was over 3.5-fold, indicating the high sensitivity towards pH changes. Furthermore, the luminescence lifetime also changed with pH variation (Fig. S13 and Table S4). Intrigued by the fact that the PL and Abs intensity of EuL were highly dependent on the pH and concentration of Zn2+ ions, we constructed a rational combination of an inhibit (INH) molecular logic gate using a hydrogen ion (H+) and Zn2+ as the chemical inputs, while the PL signals and Abs signals functioned as outputs. The presence and absence of H+ and Zn2+ were defined as 1 and 0 states, respectively. For output, the normalized PL intensity above 0.5 at 617 nm and the normalized Abs intensity above 0.5 at 325 nm were defined as 1, otherwise defined as 0. Based on such kinds of definition, we successfully constructed an inhibit (INH) molecular logic gate (integrated by combining NOT and AND gates) by monitoring the luminescence and absorbance channels ( Fig. 5). The corresponding input combinations were collected in the truth table. The other molecular logic gates (YES and OR) were also reported in Fig. 5 (including symbolisations, combinatorial logic schemes and truth tables), using H+, Fe2+ and Mn2+ as the chemical inputs, while the Abs and relaxivity signals functioned as outputs, respectively. And the absorbance curves of EuL for different combinations of the two inputs including H+ and Fe2+ was shown in Fig. S14.

To further evaluate the potential practical applications of this new molecular robot based on EuL , the sensing of metal ions in human urine was carried out. The urine sample was subjected to different dilution before analysis. Take detection of Fe2+ in the UV–vis channel and detection of Zn2+ in the PL channel for example, 10-fold diluted samples containing 20 µM EuL were spiked with various concentrations of standard Fe2+ solution and Zn2+ solution, respectively. As shown in Supplementary Table S5, good recoveries and high analytical precision were obtained, according to the previous linear relation shown in Figs. S2 and S7. These results further confirmed the reliability and feasibility of the lanthanide-based molecular robot for monitoring bio-cations in biological samples (Shen and Xia, 2014; Shangguan et al., 2017).

To conclude, we have developed a precious molecular robot prototype based on an unique europium(III)-cyclen complex in aqueous solution and human urine for the first time. This molecular robot prototype can perform the multiplex analysis for Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ by using three-channel including UV–vis, PL, and MRI, respectively. Moreover, three modes of molecular logic devices (OR, INHIBIT, and YES) were also proposed based on relevant multiplex sensing. We believe that the sample shown here can push forward the development of molecular robots, containing intelligent multi-analytes sensing and information processing at molecular level, which can be used as medical device for specific diagnostic or therapeutic functions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Projects 21471071, 21431002) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2017-Ot05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.09.029.
